title: Planning for Dialectical Abstinence Plan for Abstinence   1. Enjoy your success, but with a clear mind; plan for temptations to relapse.

pages.text:
358
From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. Permission
to photocopy this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use only (see copyright page for details).
Distress Tolerance Handout 17a (Distress Tolerance Worksheet 14)
Planning for Dialectical Abstinence
Plan for Abstinence

 1. Enjoy your success, but with a clear mind; plan for temptations to relapse.

 2.	Spend time or touch base with people who will reinforce you for abstinence.

 3.	Plan reinforcing activities to do instead of addictive behaviors.

 4.	Burn bridges: Avoid cues and high-risk situations for addictive behaviors.

 5.	Build new bridges: Develop images, smells, and mental activities (such as,
urge surfing) to compete with information associated with craving.

 6.	Find alternative ways to rebel.

 7. Publicly announce abstinence; deny any idea of lapsing to addiction.
Plan for Harm Reduction

 1. Call your therapist, sponsor, or mentor for skills coaching.

 2.	Get in contact with other effective people who can help.

 3.	Get rid of temptations; surround yourself with cues for effective behaviors.

 4.	Review skills and handouts from DBT.

 5.	Opposite action (Emotion Regulation Handout 10) can be rehearsed
to fight guilt and shame. If no other option works, go to an anonymous
meeting of any sort and publicly report your lapse.

 6.	Building mastery and coping ahead for emotional situations (Emotion
Regulation Handout 19), and checking the facts (Emotion Regulation
Handout 8), can be used to fight feelings of being out of control.

 7. Interpersonal skills (Interpersonal Effectiveness Handouts 5–7), such as
asking for help from family, friends, sponsors, ministers, or counselors,
can also be helpful. If you are isolated, help can often be found via online
support groups.

 8.	Conduct a chain analysis to analyze what prompted the lapse (General
Handouts 7, 7a).

 9.	Problem-solve right away to find a way to “get back on the wagon” and
repair any damage you have done (Emotion Regulation Handout 12).

 10. Distract yourself, self-soothe, and improve the moment.

 11. Cheerlead yourself.

 12.	Do pros and cons of stopping addictive behaviors (Distress Tolerance
Handout 5).

 13.	Stay away from extreme thinking. Don’t let one slip turn into a disaster.

 14. Recommit to 100% total abstinence.

Distress Tolerance Handout 17a (Distress Tolerance Worksheet 14)
Planning for Dialectical Abstinence
Plan for Abstinence

 1. Enjoy your success, but with a clear mind; plan for temptations to relapse.

 2.	Spend time or touch base with people who will reinforce you for abstinence.

 3.	Plan reinforcing activities to do instead of addictive behaviors.

 4.	Burn bridges: Avoid cues and high-risk situations for addictive behaviors.

 5.	Build new bridges: Develop images, smells, and mental activities (such as,
urge surfing) to compete with information associated with craving.

 6.	Find alternative ways to rebel.

 7. Publicly announce abstinence; deny any idea of lapsing to addiction.
Plan for Harm Reduction

 1. Call your therapist, sponsor, or mentor for skills coaching.

 2.	Get in contact with other effective people who can help.

 3.	Get rid of temptations; surround yourself with cues for effective behaviors.

 4.	Review skills and handouts from DBT.

 5.	Opposite action (Emotion Regulation Handout 10) can be rehearsed
to fight guilt and shame. If no other option works, go to an anonymous
meeting of any sort and publicly report your lapse.

 6.	Building mastery and coping ahead for emotional situations (Emotion
Regulation Handout 19), and checking the facts (Emotion Regulation
Handout 8), can be used to fight feelings of being out of control.

 7. Interpersonal skills (Interpersonal Effectiveness Handouts 5–7), such as
asking for help from family, friends, sponsors, ministers, or counselors,
can also be helpful. If you are isolated, help can often be found via online
support groups.

 8.	Conduct a chain analysis to analyze what prompted the lapse (General
Handouts 7, 7a).

 9.	Problem-solve right away to find a way to “get back on the wagon” and
repair any damage you have done (Emotion Regulation Handout 12).

 10. Distract yourself, self-soothe, and improve the moment.

 11. Cheerlead yourself.

 12.	Do pros and cons of stopping addictive behaviors (Distress Tolerance
Handout 5).

 13.	Stay away from extreme thinking. Don’t let one slip turn into a disaster.

 14. Recommit to 100% total abstinence.
copyright_notice: Extracted from user-provided PDF: DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition by Marsha M. Linehan. Respect source copyright and purchaser-use limits.

ourspace_kindness_pass.title: Planning for Dialectical Abstinence Plan for Abstinence   1. Enjoy your success, but with a clear mind; plan for temptations to relapse.

main_doability_needs: bed-level or seated options by default
oxygen/breathing, pain, vertigo, fatigue, mobility, and recovery time respected
ADHD/autism supports: transitions, low-stimulation starts, tiny visible steps
partial completion and stopping before a crash count as success
no shame, no body blame, no pressure to push through pain or dysphoria
ADHD transition support before task demands
caregiver burnout recovery treated as care, not avoidance
protected rest, boundaries, decompression, sensory needs, and people-pleasing checks
no assumption that Jasper has unlimited caregiver capacity

household_rules: no hour block may hold more than two tasks/tools
skills may be used as one-breath or one-field versions
survival days load critical care first and skip optional worksheets
rest, boundaries, decompression, and asking for help count as care
skills are for support, not grades

universal_kindness_rules: This is a support tool, not a test.
One line, one checkbox, one breath, or one tiny step counts.
Skipping a skill because the body is unsafe, exhausted, dysregulated, or overloaded is allowed.
Do not stack this with more than one other task inside the same hour.
Survival-day version always wins over perfection.
william_accessibility_adaptations: Use bed-level, seated, voice-note, or caregiver-assisted versions when needed.
Avoid pain escalation, respiratory strain, unsafe transfers, and dysphoria-triggering language.
Break worksheets into one-field chunks; stopping before a crash is success.
jasper_accessibility_adaptations: Start with transition support before expecting focus.
Protect caregiver burnout recovery; rest and boundaries are valid completion.
Use body-doubling, timers, low-stimulation versions, and one-minute starts.
tiny_steps: Choose one sentence or tool that fits today.
Use the low-spoon version; no full worksheet is required.

routine_integration.start_the_day_use: eligible as a rotating daily skill, never more than one DBT/ADHD skill in an hour block
routine_integration.recommended_block: after body check or before decompression
